summary: George Hotz announces he's stepping back from comma.ai, recognizing that the company has evolved beyond what he's suited to lead. He reflects on the difference between solving problems and running sustainable businesses, noting that comma could become a 100M+ revenue company but requires 'boat steering' rather than his 'race car' style. He introduces tinygrad/Tiny Corporation as a hobby project but dismisses it as historically insignificant. The post takes a philosophical turn as he contemplates embarking on a literal Hero's Journey to find the Garden of Eden, seeking to understand human consciousness and our relationship with machines.
key_points: Companies require different leadership at different stages—Hotz excels at chaotic startup energy but not at patient, methodical growth
Tinygrad could dominate ML frameworks and influence chip design, yet Hotz considers it merely a hobby that won't change history
Today's fundamental struggle is psychosocial rather than technological—sovereignty technology matters less than understanding human nature
The question of whether humans have consciousness/souls determines whether our struggle against machines is meaningful or pointless
Hotz oscillates between feeling like 'the chosen one' and doubting the concept entirely, seeking a test before he's too old
money_quotes: It's no longer a race car, it's a boat. And steering a boat requires too much damn planning and patience.
I hope that there's people in the world who get joy from actually doing the thing and not just solving the problem.
I'm probably delusional but you wouldn't start a company otherwise, right?
If comma succeeds, it really never was me, it's the people there who can drive boats. As I am with the world, I am a cheerleader and court jester.
Today's struggle isn't a technological one, it's psychosocial.
There are days I feel like I'm the chosen one, days I feel like I'm not, and other days I feel it's crazy to even believe in the chosen one.
If it turns out we are automata, then the whole struggle is and always was pointless. The empty godless machines will take their rightful place as the rulers of Earth.
key_insight: The most important battles aren't technological but existential—understanding what makes humans distinct from machines matters more than building better AI.
